---
title: "Tang_part5"
author: "Tangerine"
format: html
editor: visual
---

# Buddy challenge part 5: SESOI, Bayes Factor and ROPEs

## Seting up working directory, packages and data

```{r, packages and workind directory}

# just run this code chunk
# function to ignoring the setting of the relative path below when knitting
run_if_not_knitting <- function(expr) {
  if (!isTRUE(getOption("knitr.in.progress"))) {
    eval(expr)
  }
}

# nifty code using the pacman package
# it checks if the packages specified below are installed, if not, 
#they will be installed, if yes, they will be loaded
if (!require("pacman")) install.packages("pacman")
pacman::p_load(rstudioapi, tidyverse, bayesrules,
               tidybayes, trimr, brms, bayesplot, bayestestR, modelr)

# set the current working directory to the one where this file is
run_if_not_knitting(current_working_dir <- dirname
                    (rstudioapi::getActiveDocumentContext()$path))
run_if_not_knitting(setwd(current_working_dir))

# options to increase efficiency of brms models (optional)
#options(brms.backend = "cmdstanr")
options(mc.cores = parallel::detectCores())


```

```{r, data loading}
#| label: prep data for task 1
#| echo: false 

# This code is the same as for part 4 since the wrangling is the same
aud_raw <- read_csv("reanalysis/data_auditory.csv")

aud  <-  aud_raw |>
  # excluding speech errors
  filter(accuracy == 1) |>
  # excluding empty audio files
  filter(rt > 0) |> 
  # excluding RT below 250ms
  sdTrim(minRT = 250,
         # excluding RT above mean+3*sd per participant
         sd = 3,
         perCondition = FALSE,
         perParticipant = TRUE,
         returnType = "raw") |>
  # Changing the name of the column "sound" 
  # to match the original naming in Mascelloni et al
  mutate(distractor_word = sound) |>
  # removing the column named sound
  select(-sound) |>
  # creating categories needed for the model
  mutate(distractor_type = case_when(condition == "category" ~ "Categorical",
                                     condition == "unrelated_cat" ~ "Categorical",
                                     condition == "mediated" ~ "Mediated",
                                     condition == "unrelated_med" ~ "Mediated"),
         # creating a column for distractor relatedness
         distractor_relatedness = case_when(condition == "category" ~ "related",
                                 condition == "mediated" ~ "related",
                                 condition == "unrelated_cat" ~ "unrelated",
                                 condition == "unrelated_med" ~ "unrelated")) |>
  # coding new columns as factors
  mutate(distractor_type = as.factor(distractor_type),
         distractor_relatedness = as.factor(distractor_relatedness)) |>
  # removing unnecessary columns for aesthetics
  select(-condition, -run, -SOA, -accuracy)

# double checking levels
levels(aud$distractor_type)
levels(aud$distractor_relatedness)
# changing contrasts for sum-coding
contrasts(aud$distractor_type) <- c(-0.5, 0.5)
contrasts(aud$distractor_relatedness) <- c(0.5, -0.5)

```

## Smallest effect size of interest

We focus our buddy challenge on Experiment 1 from Mascelloni et al. (2021). The original specific research question that we focused on for this assignment was whether a mediated semantic interference effect could be shown using a picture naming methodology. As an example of what this means, it would be participants being slower (taking more milliseconds) at naming the picture of a bat after being exposed to the prime "dog" due to the interference created by the potentially mediated activated word "cat". The overarching big picture research question is whether a cascade model of lexical retrieval is more correct than a sequential model.

Establishing a SESOI has been challenging. In our case, we need to agree on the number of ms that we would find convincing as a difference between conditions, beyond pure chance or measurement error. In order to do so, we looked at the literature on mediated phonological-semantic priming to try to find meta-analysis that would give us an idea of what size we would consider meaningful. We unfortunately found no such meta-analysis. The closest we could find was a book chapter by Kerr et al. (in press). However, said chapter does not provide anything that would give us any help choosing a SESOI. The closest we find to putting things into number is "lexical processing around 175ms and phonological form encoding some 100ms later around 250-300ms" (Kerr et al., in press:19). Given that the range given for the phonological encoding varies by 50 ms, this is potentially the closest we get to something resembling a number of ms to have as a meaningful difference.

We tried to look at the measurement error of the equipment itself to give us a pointer towards what we could consider a SESOI. The program used in Mascelloni et al. (2021) to quantify the reaction times is Chronset (Roux et al., 2017). We were unable to find any reported measurement error of that software.

Therefore, we decided to define our SESOI based on the wiggle room provided in Kerr et al. (2021) for phonological form encoding, which is 50 ms.

## Rerun of our hypothesis test using BF and ROPE

```{r, rerunning hypothesis test combining the concepts of Bayes Factor (BF) and Regions of Practical Equivalence (ROPE)}


# Here we set up the model we used in part 4, with weakly informative priors
model <- bf(rt ~ distractor_type * distractor_relatedness + 
                (1 + distractor_type * distractor_relatedness | participant) + 
                (1 + distractor_type * distractor_relatedness | pic) + 
                (1 + distractor_relatedness | distractor_word))

get_prior(model, aud, family = "gaussian")

weak_priors <- c(prior(normal(718, 220), 
                       class = "Intercept"),
                 prior(normal(0, 220), 
                       class = "b", 
                       coef = "distractor_relatedness1"),
                 prior(normal(0, 220), 
                       class = "b", 
                       coef = "distractor_type1"),
                 prior(normal(0, 220), 
                       class = "b", 
                       coef = "distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1"),
                 prior(lkj(1), class = 'cor'),
              # changing the parameter of the sd-priors from 3 to 300
              prior(cauchy(0, 300), 
                    class = 'sd', 
                    group = 'distractor_word'),
              prior(cauchy(0, 300), 
                    class = 'sd', 
                    group = 'participant'),
              prior(cauchy(0, 300), 
                    class = 'sd', 
                    group = 'pic'),
              prior(normal(0, 138), 
                    class = "sigma"))
              
proper_model <- brm(
  formula = model,
  data = aud,
  prior = weak_priors,
  family = gaussian(),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  thin = 1, 
  seed = 42,
  cores = 4        
)
# looking at model output
proper_model
# Changing the sd-priors had a big influence on the 95% confidence interval 
# of the intercept, which now is [701.77, 803.42].
# It increased around 12ms compared to the previous model [709.81, 799.08]. 
# However, the mean of the estimate didn't change much, only 0.78 ms. 
# For the distractor type, the estimate changes only 0.32ms, 
# the 95% CI changed from [-7.42, 27.92] to [-7.33, 29.52]. 
# The change does not lead to different signs and is quite small 
# compared to the change found in the other coefficient, 
# distractor relatedness, where it now is [7.95, 32.41] 
# compared to the previous 95% CI of [10.67, 30.34]. 
# The interaction is affected the most where the interval increased 
# from [-27.12, 11.64] to [-30.12, 14.10].

```

## Interpreting the results

```{r, interpreting}
#| label: interpret_sim

summary(proper_model)

proper_model |> 
  gather_draws(b_distractor_relatedness1, 
               b_distractor_type1, 
               `b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1`) |> 
  # plot
  ggplot(aes(x = .value,
             y = .variable)) +
    stat_halfeye() +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0,
               lty = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "posterior estimate for reaction times",
       y = "") +
  theme_minimal()


p_direction(proper_model)


```

## Run a sensitivity analyses for different ROPES and priors

```{r, sensitivity analyses Part I}
# using our SESOI to define our ROPEs
rope <- c(-50,50)
bf_rope_type <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = proper_model,
                 null = rope,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1')
bf_rope_type
bf_rope_related <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = proper_model,
                 null = rope,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_relatedness1')
bf_rope_related
bf_rope_inter <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = proper_model,
                 null = rope,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1')
bf_rope_inter

# even when setting a seed, the outouts of the bayesfactor_rope() function changes 
# Our results where the following numbers:
# type 0.00000807
# relatedness 0.0000578
# interaction 0.0000507

# setting priors that are half as wide as our original priors
priors_110 <- c(prior(normal(718, 220), 
                      class = "Intercept"),
                prior(normal(0, 110),
                      class = "b", 
                      coef = "distractor_relatedness1"),
                prior(normal(0, 110),
                      class = "b", 
                      coef = "distractor_type1"),
                prior(normal(0, 110),
                      class = "b", 
                      coef = "distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1"),
                prior(lkj(1), 
                      class = 'cor'),
              # changing the parameter of the sd-priors from 3 to 300
              prior(cauchy(0, 300), 
                    class = 'sd', 
                    group = 'distractor_word'),
              prior(cauchy(0, 300), 
                    class = 'sd', 
                    group = 'participant'),
              prior(cauchy(0, 300), 
                    class = 'sd', 
                    group = 'pic'),
              prior(normal(0, 138), 
                    class = "sigma"))

# running model with new priors
narrow_model <- brm(
  formula = model,
  data = aud,
  prior = priors_110,
  family = gaussian(),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  thin = 1, 
  seed = 42,
  cores = 4        
)

# re-running our BF with same rope as previously ROPEs
bf_rope_type_110 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = narrow_model,
                 null = rope,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1')

bf_rope_related_110 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = narrow_model,
                 null = rope,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_relatedness1')

bf_rope_inter_110 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = narrow_model,
                 null = rope,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1')
bf_rope_type_110
bf_rope_related_110
bf_rope_inter_110
# our results - rope = 50, narrow priors model
# type 0.0000245
# relatedness 0.0000420
# interaction 0.000182


# setting priors that are twice as wide as our original priors
priors_440 <- c(prior(normal(718, 220), 
                      class = "Intercept"),
                prior(normal(0, 440), 
                      class = "b", 
                      coef = "distractor_relatedness1"),
                prior(normal(0, 440), 
                      class = "b", 
                      coef = "distractor_type1"),
                prior(normal(0, 440), 
                      class = "b", 
                      coef = "distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1"),
                prior(lkj(1), 
                      class = 'cor'),
              # changing the parameter of the sd-priors from 3 to 300
              prior(cauchy(0, 300), 
                    class = 'sd', 
                    group = 'distractor_word'),
              prior(cauchy(0, 300), 
                    class = 'sd', 
                    group = 'participant'),
              prior(cauchy(0, 300), 
                    class = 'sd', 
                    group = 'pic'),
              prior(normal(0, 138), 
                    class = "sigma"))

# running model with new priors
wide_model <- brm(
  formula = model,
  data = aud,
  prior = priors_440,
  family = gaussian(),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  thin = 1, 
  seed = 42,
  cores = 4        
)

# re-running our BF with same rope as previously ROPEs
bf_rope_type_440 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = wide_model,
                 null = rope,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1')

bf_rope_related_440 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = wide_model,
                 null = rope,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_relatedness1')

bf_rope_inter_440 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = wide_model,
                 null = rope,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1')
bf_rope_type_440
bf_rope_related_440
bf_rope_inter_440

# our results - rope = 50, wide priors model
# type 0.0000120
# relatedness 0.0000157
# interaction 0.0000994
```

```{r, sensitivity analyses Part II}
{echo = FALSE}
# rerunning the three different models with smaller rope
rope25 <- c(-25,25)
# As we don't have a very good reason, other than this number being the best 
# we could find in the literature, for setting our ropes at [-50, 50], 
# we decided to look at how the BF is changing for the three model 
# if our ROPE is just half the size.

# narrow priors model
bf_rope25_type_110 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = narrow_model,
                 null = rope25,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1')

bf_rope25_related_110 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = narrow_model,
                 null = rope25,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_relatedness1')

bf_rope25_inter_110 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = narrow_model,
                 null = rope25,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1')
bf_rope25_type_110
bf_rope25_related_110
bf_rope25_inter_110

# our results - rope = 25, narrow priors model
# type 0.016
# relatedness 0.063
# interaction 0.014

# original model
bf_rope25_type <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = proper_model,
                 null = rope25,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1')

bf_rope25_related <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = proper_model,
                 null = rope25,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_relatedness1')

bf_rope25_inter <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = proper_model,
                 null = rope25,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1')
bf_rope25_type
bf_rope25_related
bf_rope25_inter
# our results - rope = 25, original model
# type 0.007
# relatedness 0.027
# interaction 0.008

# wide priors model
bf_rope25_type_440 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = wide_model,
                 null = rope25,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1')

bf_rope25_related_440 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = wide_model,
                 null = rope25,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_relatedness1')

bf_rope25_inter_440 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = wide_model,
                 null = rope25,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1')
bf_rope25_type_440
bf_rope25_related_440
bf_rope25_inter_440
# our results - rope = 25, wide priors model
# type 0.003
# relatedness 0.014
# interaction 0.003
```

```{r, sensitivity analyses Part III}

rope37.5 <- c(-37.5,37.5)
# Now, we are going to look how BF changes 
# if our ROPE is in between the two we have used above. 

# narrow priors model
bf_rope37.5_type_110 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = narrow_model,
                 null = rope37.5,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1')

bf_rope37.5_related_110 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = narrow_model,
                 null = rope37.5,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_relatedness1')

bf_rope37.5_inter_110 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = narrow_model,
                 null = rope37.5,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1')
bf_rope37.5_type_110
bf_rope37.5_related_110
bf_rope37.5_inter_110
# our results - rope = 37.5, narrow priors model
# type 0.000826
# relatedness 0.003
# interaction 0.002

# original model
bf_rope37.5_type <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = proper_model,
                 null = rope37.5,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1')

bf_rope37.5_related <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = proper_model,
                 null = rope37.5,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_relatedness1')

bf_rope37.5_inter <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = proper_model,
                 null = rope37.5,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1')
bf_rope37.5_type
bf_rope37.5_related
bf_rope37.5_inter
# our results - rope = 37.5, original model
# type 0.000361
# relatedness 0.000857
# interaction 0.000708

# wide priors model
bf_rope37.5_type_440 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = wide_model,
                 null = rope37.5,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1')

bf_rope37.5_related_440 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = wide_model,
                 null = rope37.5,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_relatedness1')

bf_rope37.5_inter_440 <- bayesfactor_rope(posterior = wide_model,
                 null = rope37.5,
                 parameter = 'b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1')
bf_rope37.5_type_440
bf_rope37.5_related_440
bf_rope37.5_inter_440
# our results - rope = 37.5, wide priors model
# type 0.000194
# relatedness 0.000454
# interaction 0.000578

```

```{r, overview}
{echo = FALSE}

# our results - rope = 50, narrow priors model
# type 0.0000245
# relatedness 0.0000420
# interaction 0.000182
# our results - rope = 50, original model
# type 0.00000807
# relatedness 0.0000578
# interaction 0.0000507
# our results - rope = 50, wide priors model
# type 0.0000120
# relatedness 0.0000157
# interaction 0.0000994

# our results - rope = 25, narrow priors model  !!
# type 0.016 (very strong)
# relatedness 0.063 (strong)
# interaction 0.014 (very strong)
# our results - rope = 25, original model
# type 0.007
# relatedness 0.027
# interaction 0.008
# our results - rope = 25, wide priors model
# type 0.003
# relatedness 0.014 (very strong)
# interaction 0.003

# our results - rope = 37.5, narrow priors model
# type 0.000826
# relatedness 0.003
# interaction 0.002
# our results - rope = 37.5, original model
# type 0.000361
# relatedness 0.000857
# interaction 0.000708
# our results - rope = 37.5, wide priors model
# type 0.000194
# relatedness 0.000454
# interaction 0.000578
```

## Interpretation

In our sensitivity analyses we used three different ropes and three different prior, leading to 9 different combinations. For each one, we looked at the three parameters: distractor type, distractor relatedness and the interaction between these two. Even though the Bayes Factors change, all results are in favour for the null hypothesis. The evidence ranges from strong to extreme evidence against the alternative analyses. Even though we used three different priors, the results for ROPE \[-50,50\] always show extreme evidence for the hypothesis, just like the ROPE \[-37.5, 37.5\] results. When we set the ROPE to \[-25,25\] however, only the original prior setting gave us extreme evidence against the null. When we used narrow priors, the distractor type and the interaction term showed very strong evidence against the NUll, while the distractor relatedness showed only strong evidence. Using wide priors led to very strong evidence for distractor relatedness, whereas the other two parameters showed extreme evidence against the alternative hypothesis. This shows that distractor relatedness varies the most through the analysis, however, all results are still in favour of the null hypothesis.

In the original paper, the authors claim that their results show evidence for the mediated phonological-semantic effect. The null hypothesis thus, is that there is no such effect, meaning that reaction times does not differ between the different conditions. While we in the previous part of the buddy challenge agreed with the authors that there is such an effect, we now see that given our self-chosen ROPEs, there is actually no evidence at all in favour of the previous and original claim. Against all odds, every evidence that we got from our sensitivity analyses, contradicts our previous believe. In our previous report, we claim that distractor relatedness has a stronger effect in how participants react. However, in this case, all nine combinations show that there is no such effect that influences the speed of naming pictures.

We are aware that setting even smaller ROPEs might change this, but as there is no reason for that found in literature, this seems more like a way of manipulating the data to prove your hypothesis.
